source_metadata.title_raw: Cedrick L. Hicks Sr. - A-19-142 - September 4, 2019 - Carson

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Cedrick L. Hicks, Sr. 
701 East Carson Street· 
Carson, CA 90745 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-142 
Dear Mr. Hicks: 
September 4,2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift limit and conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Pl~ase note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does a four-year academic scholarship that your daughter received from California 
State University Dominguez Hills ("CSUDH") for the purpose of attending college at CSUDH 
constitute a "gift" to you that is subject to the Act's $500 annual limit on gifts to public 
officials? 
2. Does the academic scholarship create a financial interest that disqualifies you from 
participating in or influencing any decision of the Carson City Council regarding the potential 
reduction or elimination of services provided by the City of Carson's passport office, given that 
CSUDH also has a passport office located in close proximity to the City's passport office? Does 
your former role as an Alumni Advisor for CSUDH impact the analysis or change the conclusion? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-19-142 
Page No. 2 
1. The scholarship is not a gift to you under the Act only if the scholarship is reported as 
income. While a scholarship to your daughter from CSUDH is potentially a gift to you, the 
~cholarship is not a gift to the extent that it was earned by your daughter through a bona fide 
competition, and you report the scholarship as income. 
2. No. While you have a potentially disqualifying interest in CSUDH as a source of income, 
it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision regarding the City Hall passport office will have a 
material financial effect on your interest in CSUDH or your personal finances. Your former position 
as uncompensated Alumni Advisory Councilmember has no bearing on this analysis and does not 
change this conclusion. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are an elected Councilmember and Mayor Pro Tern for the City of Carson. Your 
daughter, who is seventeen years old and a dependent as listed on your federal tax returns, recently 
applied for and was awarded a four-year academic scholarship to attend CSUDH as an 
undergraduate student on a full-time basis. CSUDH is located in the City of Carson. The academic 
scholarship that CSUDH awarded to your daughter has a total value of approximately $33,000 over 
four years. The academic scholarship will be disbursed on a semester schedule, based on proof of 
your daughter maintaining a cumulative grade point average ("GPA") of 3 .0 or higher. Your 
daughter will continue to live at home with you and her mother for the first year she attends 
CSUDH, and thereafter will reside in campus housing. 
Applying for the academic scholarship requires submission of a written essay and a 
recommendation letter from a school counselor or teacher, as well as proof of grades and 
information on community involvement. Once the application is submitted, an interview is 
co~ducted by a CSUDH Academic Scholarship Committee panel. Your daughter submitted all of 
the required information and participated in the required interview. 
All high school or college transfer students are eligible to apply for the academic 
scholarship, but there is a minimum requirement that an applicant must have a GPA of 3.5 or better 
to receive the academic scholarship. The academic scholarship is awarded based on the grades and 
community involvement of the applicant. 
You also note that you served on the CSUDH Alumni Advisory Council as an "Alumni 
Advisor" for approximately 16 months, from about October of2017 to February of 2019. You were 
appointed to the position by vote of CSUDH's Alumni Council. Your duties as a CSUDH Alumni 
Advisor were to recruit alumni of CSUDH to be involved in being mentors to current students, to 
speak to students about attending CSUDH, to promote CSUDH to the State Board of Trustees, and 
to act as an advisor to the CSUDH faculty about trends revealed in studies from the CSUDH student 
body. You no longer serve in any official role, position or capacity for CSUDH. In a follow-up 
email, you noted that you did not receive any compensation, salary, reimbursement for expenses, or 
per diem for your service on the on the Alumni Advisory Council, as this was a voluntary position. 


File No. A-19-142 
Page No. 3 
CSUDH has a passport office which provides services to the public related to processing 
of passport applications. The passport office generates ( or is capable of generating) revenue for 
CSUDH, to the extent the statutory fees received by CSUDH for providing such services exceed the 
operating costs of the passport office. The City of Carson also has a passport office, which is 
located at City Hall. The Carson City Hall passport office also provides services to the public 
related to processing of passport applications, and generates, or is capable of generating, revenue 
for the City of Carson in the same manner that the CSUDH passport office generates revenue for 
CSUDH. The CSUDH passport office is located less than four miles from Carson City Hall so the 
two passport offices are presumed to be competitors. 
The Carson City Council may soon make decisions regarding the future of the Carson City 
Hall passport office, including whether, when and/or to what extent the services it provides will be 
reduced or terminated. The CSUDH passport office (and thus CSUDH) may stand to financially 
benefit from a reduction or termination in services provided by the Carson City Hall passport office, 
because at least some people who would otherwise seek the services of the Carson City Hall 
passport office presumably would instead go to the CSUDH passport office, increasing the demand 
for services at the CSUDH passport office. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public. official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from the decisions effect on the public generally, on one or more of the official's 
financial interests. (Section 87103.) 
Under the Act financial interests include: 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least 
$2,000. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at 
least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
• 
Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received 
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income 
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public 
official within 12 months before the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
Any donor of gift(s) amounting to a total of at least $460 within 12 months before a decision 
is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
• 
A public official's own personal finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate 
family. (Section 87103).) 


Academic Scholarship 
File No. A-19-142 
Page No. 4 
The Act defines "gift" in Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal benefit 
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and 
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made 
in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status." Under 
some circumstances, a gift to an official's immediate family member is considered a gift to the 
official. Immediate family includes dependent children (See Regulation 18943). In cases where a 
payment is made to and accepted and received by you or your daughter, Regulation 18943(c) 
provides that the gifts will be considered gifts to you if either of the following applies: 
(1) There is no established working, social, or similar relationship between 
the donor and the family member that would suggest an association between 
the donor and the family member suitable or appropriate for making the type 
of payment provided to the family member. 
(2) There is evidence to suggest the donor had a purpose to influence the 
official.2 
In this case, the CSUDH, as an educational institution located within the City of Carson, is 
an entity that will likely come before the City Council for multiple purposes. Considering the 
likelihood that the CSUDH may be implicated by any number of City Council decisions, there is · 
evidence to suggest that the CSUDH may have a purpose to influence you as a public official and 
the actions of the City Council. Based on these facts, the academic scholarship is potentially a gift 
to you under Regulation 18943, as opposed to a gift to your daughter. However, our analysis does 
not end there. The Act's broad definition of gift has several exceptions to allow for day-to-day 
business and practicalities of public office. 
The "bona fide competition" is an exception to the "gift" rule. (Regulation 18942(a)(14).) A 
scholarship or other prize received by a public official in• a bona fide competition, rather than with 
regard to his or her official status, is not a gift and is reported as income. (Regulation 18942(a)(14).) 
The scholarship must be the same for the public official (or his or her immediate family) as would 
be offered any others. One of the main factors in making this determinatiol} is the nature of the pool 
of contestants in the competition and the specific circumstances of the award. For instance, in a 
previous advice letter, we advised that a "Woman of the Year" award provided to Assemblymember 
Jackie Speier qualified as a bona fide competition unrelated to her official status since an 
independent panel of judges selected her from of a large pool of contestants representing the 
2 Evidence to suggest the donor had a purpose to influence the official include arty of the following 
circumstances: (1) the payment is made to a family member ofa state or local government agency official by a donor, 
or the donor's agent, if the donor is involved in an action or decision before the official's agency, in which the official 
will reasonably foreseeably participate or in an action or decision in which he or she has participated within the last 12 
months; or (2) the payment is made to a family member by a person who has a contract with the official's agency or,by 
a person who engages in a business that regularly seeks contracts with or comes before the agency for the purpose of 
receiving a license, permit, or other entitlement and the official may reasonably foreseeably make or participate in a 
governmental decision, as defined in the Act's conflict of interest regulations, related to the person, or has participated 
in any decision related to the person within 12 months of the time the gift is made. 


File No. A-19-142 
Page No. 5 
fields of medicine, sports, social work, journalism and public service. (Pritchard Advice Letter, No. 
A-95-094; See also Maitland Advice Letter, A-99-158 [official was advised that a fellowship 
offered to her as a mid-career professional in a public policy field was an award received in a bona 
fide competition].) 
· 
In this case, your daughter applied for a scholarship after meeting specified academic 
criteria. You state that all high school or college transfer students are eligible to apply for the 
academic scholarship, and that there is a minimum GP A requirement as a part of the qualification 
process. The academic scholarship is awarded based on the grades and community involvement of 
the applicant. 
' 
Based on the facts you provided, the academic scholarship appears to qualify as an award 
received in a bona fide competition. First, the application process was open to all high school or 
college transfer students with minimum GP As. Second, the application process was a competitive 
process. Applicants were required to submit a written essay and a recommendation letter from a 
school counselor or teacher, as well as proof of grades and information on community involvement. 
A CSUDH Academic Scholarship Committee panel then conducted interviews of applicants. 
Therefore, the scholarship is not considered to be a gift to her or you under the Regulation 
18942(a)(14) if you report the scholarship as income. 
· 
City Hall Passport Office Decision 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) As 
noted above, an official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest are 
identified in Section 87103, and subdivision (c) of that section provides that an official has an 
interest in a source of income aggregating $500 or more to the official in the 12 months prior to the 
decision at issue. 
While you do not have a financial interest in CSUDH as an uncompensated former member 
of the Alumni Advisory Council, you do have an interest in CSUDH as a source of income resulting 
for the scholarship received by your daughter. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
In order to determine whether you have a conflict of interest regarding decisions regarding 
the future of the Carson City Hall passport office, we consider whether the effect of the decisions 
on your source of income and personal finances would be reasonably .foreseeable. Under the Act, an 
effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and 
an interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the decision. 
(Regulation 18701(a).) In this case, your source of income and personal finances are not explicitly 
involved in the decision on the future of the Carson City Hall passport office. Therefore, the effect 
of the decision on your source of income and personal finances is reasonably foreseeable if it can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 
18701(b).) 


Source of Income 
File No. A-19-142 
Page No. 6 
As stated above an official is disqualified from a decision only if the decisions will have an 
effect on the official's interests distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 
87103.) With respect to income received by an official from a governmental entity, such as the 
receipt of the scholarship for CSUDH, Regulation 18702.3(d) provides that "an official with an 
interest in a governmental entity is disqualified from taking part in a decision only if there is a 
unique effect on the official" pursuant to Regulation 18703(e)(7).3 Under Regulation 18703(e)(7), 
an effect on a governmental entity that is a source of income is deemed indistinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally, so long as "[t]he decision affects a federal, state, or local 
governmental entity in which the official has an interest and there is no unique effect on the 
official's interest." 
In this case, you note that a reduction or termination of passport services offered at the City 
Hall passport office may affect the CSUDH passport office because at least some people who would 
otherwise seek the services of the Carson City Hall passport office presumably would instead go to 
the CSUDH passport office. However, the effect of the decision will be limited to CSUDH and the 
public in general. It is not foreseeable that the decision will have any unique effect on your interest 
in CSUDH based upon the receipt of a scholarship. 
Personal Finances 
Regulation 18702.S(a) provides for the materiality standard for a financial interest in a 
personal financial effect as follows: 
A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental 
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate 
family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's 
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss 
from the decision. 
You note that decisions to reduce or eliminate services at the Carson City Hall passport 
office may increase the demand for services at the CSUDH passport office, as people who would 
have gone to City Hall for passport services would instead go to the CSUDH office. However, to 
the extent that this decision· may have any effect on the CSUDH passport office, there would be no 
effect on your personal finances based on these facts. Therefore, you would not have a financial 
interest in decisions involving the City Hall passport office. 
3 Regulation 18702.3 was recently amended by the Commission and has been submitted to the Office of 
Administrative Law for posting in the California Code of Regulations. Although not currently effective, the regulation, 
as amended, expresses the Commission's policy concerning application of the conflict of interest rules of the Act. 
Therefore, staff is advising consistent with the Commission's policy. 


File No. A-19-142 
Page No. 7 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
3.tY!k-ff 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

material financial effect on your interest in CSUDH or your personal finances. Your former position 
as uncompensated Alumni Advisory Councilmember has no bearing on this analysis and does not 
change this conclusion.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public. official has a "financial interest" in a governmental

$2,000. (Section 87103(b).)

least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,

• 
Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received 
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income

family. (Section 87103).)

provides that the gifts will be considered gifts to you if either of the following applies:

the donor and the family member that would suggest an association between

official.2

will reasonably foreseeably participate or in an action or decision in which he or she has participated within the last 12

a person who engages in a business that regularly seeks contracts with or comes before the agency for the purpose of

While you do not have a financial interest in CSUDH as an uncompensated former member 
of the Alumni Advisory Council, you do have an interest in CSUDH as a source of income resulting 
for the scholarship received by your daughter.

In order to determine whether you have a conflict of interest regarding decisions regarding 
the future of the Carson City Hall passport office, we consider whether the effect of the decisions 
on your source of income and personal finances would be reasonably .foreseeable. Under the Act, an 
effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and 
an interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the decision.

As stated above an official is disqualified from a decision only if the decisions will have an

an effect on a governmental entity that is a source of income is deemed indistinguishable from the

official's interest." 
In this case, you note that a reduction or termination of passport services offered at the City

otherwise seek the services of the Carson City Hall passport office presumably would instead go to

in CSUDH based upon the receipt of a scholarship. 
Personal Finances 
Regulation 18702.S(a) provides for the materiality standard for a financial interest in a 
personal financial effect as follows: 
A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental 
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate 
family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's 
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss 
from the decision. 
You note that decisions to reduce or eliminate services at the Carson City Hall passport 
office may increase the demand for services at the CSUDH passport office, as people who would 
have gone to City Hall for passport services would instead go to the CSUDH office. However, to 
the extent that this decision· may have any effect on the CSUDH passport office, there would be no 
effect on your personal finances based on these facts. Therefore, you would not have a financial 
interest in decisions involving the City Hall passport office. 
3 Regulation 18702.3 was recently amended by the Commission and has been submitted to the Office of 
Administrative Law for posting in the California Code of Regulations. Although not currently effective, the regulation, 
as amended, expresses the Commission's policy concerning application of the conflict of interest rules of the Act. 
Therefore, staff is advising consistent with the Commission's policy.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does a four-year academic scholarship that your daughter received from California 
State University Dominguez Hills ("CSUDH") for the purpose of attending college at CSUDH 
constitute a "gift" to you that is subject to the Act's $500 annual limit on gifts to public 
officials? 
2. Does the academic scholarship create a financial interest that disqualifies you from 
participating in or influencing any decision of the Carson City Council regarding the potential 
reduction or elimination of services provided by the City of Carson's passport office, given that 
CSUDH also has a passport office located in close proximity to the City's passport office? Does 
your former role as an Alumni Advisor for CSUDH impact the analysis or change the conclusion?

CONCLUSION: 1. The scholarship is not a gift to you under the Act only if the scholarship is reported as 
income. While a scholarship to your daughter from CSUDH is potentially a gift to you, the 
~cholarship is not a gift to the extent that it was earned by your daughter through a bona fide 
competition, and you report the scholarship as income. 
2. No. While you have a potentially disqualifying interest in CSUDH as a source of income, 
it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision regarding the City Hall passport office will have a 
material financial effect on your interest in CSUDH or your personal finances. Your former position 
as uncompensated Alumni Advisory Councilmember has no bearing on this analysis and does not 
change this conclusion.

Hicks, Sr. 701 East Carson Street· Carson, CA 90745 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-142 Dear Mr. Hicks: September 4,2019 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift limit and conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Pl~ase note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS 1. Does a four-year academic scholarship that your daughter received from California State University Dominguez Hills ("CSUDH") for the purpose of attending college at CSUDH constitute a "gift" to you that is subject to the Act's $500 annual limit on gifts to public officials? 2. Does the academic scholarship create a financial interest that disqualifies you from participating in or influencing any decision of the Carson City Council regarding the potential reduction or elimination of services provided by the City of Carson's passport office, given that CSUDH also has a passport office located in close proximity to the City's passport office? Does your former role as an Alumni Advisor for CSUDH impact the analysis or change the conclusion? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSIONS File No. A-19-142 Page No. 2 1. The scholarship is not a gift to you under the Act only if the scholarship is reported as income. While a scholarship to your daughter from CSUDH is potentially a gift to you, the ~cholarship is not a gift to the extent that it was earned by your daughter through a bona fide competition, and you report the scholarship as income. 2. No. While you have a potentially disqualifying interest in CSUDH as a source of income, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision regarding the City Hall passport office will have a material financial effect on your interest in CSUDH or your personal finances. Your former position as uncompensated Alumni Advisory Councilmember has no bearing on this analysis and does not change this conclusion.
